channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: From secondhand smoke to secondhand quitting
item.description: The harms of smoking do not stop with the smoker — and neither do the benefits of quitting. When adults move away from combustible cigarettes, families and communities can see reduced exposure to smoke, odour and tobacco-related risks. Harm reduction extends that logic by supporting realistic routes away from smoking, including lower-risk smoke-free alternatives. Public health should recognise not only secondhand smoke, but the wider benefits of secondhand quitting. (Published on June 19, 2026byamericanthinker.com)

item.title: Tobacco Harm Reduction IS Harm Reduction Part One
item.description: Tobacco harm reduction belongs within the wider harm reduction movement. The goal is not to celebrate nicotine use, but to reduce the disease and death caused by combustible cigarettes. When adult smokers switch to lower-risk alternatives, public health gains are possible even without complete nicotine abstinence. A consistent harm reduction approach should prioritise safer choices, accurate information and respect for real-world behaviour. (Published on June 19, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Fruit-flavoured vapes lead UK stop-smoking services as quit rates beat other support
item.description: Fruit-flavoured vapes are playing a major role in UK stop smoking services, with quit rates reportedly outperforming other forms of support. For many adult smokers, flavours are not a gimmick — they can make lower-risk alternatives more acceptable and help distance users from the taste of cigarettes. Restricting flavours may weaken one of the tools currently helping people quit combustible tobacco. Evidence-based policy should protect young people without removing options that support adult smoking cessation. (Published on June 19, 2026byclearingtheair.eu)

item.title: European Parliament rejects EU tobacco tax report
item.description: The European Parliament’s rejection of the EU tobacco tax report deepens the deadlock over how nicotine products should be taxed. While higher cigarette taxes can support public health goals, applying steep or poorly differentiated taxes to smoke-free alternatives risks weakening incentives for smokers to switch. Nicotine taxation should reflect relative risk, not treat all products as if they cause the same harm. Better policy would target combustible tobacco while keeping lower-risk options affordable, legal and regulated. (Published on June 19, 2026bytobaccojournal.com)

item.title: THR Unfiltered #1 | Navigating the prohibitionist landscape of THR in the States #GFN26
item.description: Tobacco harm reduction in the United States is shaped by a complex mix of prohibitionist pressure, strict regulation and uneven enforcement. While adult smokers continue to need realistic alternatives to combustible cigarettes, access to safer nicotine products is often restricted by policies that prioritise fear over relative risk. The result is a market where legal pathways are difficult, illicit supply can grow and consumers receive confusing messages. A better approach would protect young people while keeping harm reduction visible, regulated and accessible. (Published on June 19, 2026bygfn.tv)
